**Oracle:** tier2-external-spec — https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/logs.html and https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_log_config.html

**Artifact shape:** CLI tool reading log lines from stdin or file path, emitting one JSON record per line to stdout (NDJSON)

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a command-line log parser that ingests web-server access logs and emits structured JSON records, one per input line (NDJSON on stdout). The tool must be invokable as `logparse [--format FORMAT] [FILE]`, reading from the given FILE or stdin when no file argument is given. The `--format` flag and the positional FILE argument may appear in either order. The tool must natively support the Apache Common Log Format (CLF) and Combined Log Format out of the box, auto-detecting which is in use when no `--format` flag is given (try Combined first; "failed Combined parse" means the regex/grammar for Combined did not match the entire line — in that case retry the line against CLF; if CLF also fails, emit an error record per the rules below). The tool may be implemented in any language; no language is preferred. No network access, no external services. Package it as a single CLI entry point.

NORMATIVE OUTPUT FIELDS (names are tested verbatim). For each successfully parsed line emit a JSON object with:
- `remote_host` (string) — may be an IPv4 address, IPv6 address (e.g. `::1`, `2001:db8::1`), or hostname; preserve as-is.
- `remote_logname` (string or null)
- `remote_user` (string or null)
- `timestamp` (string) — ISO-8601 with the ORIGINAL UTC offset encoded as a colon-separated suffix in the same string. Apache writes offsets as `-0700`; the emitter MUST reformat to `-07:00` (insert a colon between hours and minutes). Example: `[10/Oct/2000:13:55:36 -0700]` becomes `"2000-10-10T13:55:36-07:00"`. An offset of `+0000` MAY be serialized as either `+00:00` or `Z` — both are accepted. Do not normalize non-zero offsets to UTC; the offset must be recoverable by reading the suffix of this single string field. No separate offset field.
- `request_method`, `request_path`, `request_protocol` (strings) — produced by splitting the `%r` request line on the first and last spaces when it has exactly three whitespace-separated tokens. If the request line cannot be split that way, OMIT these three fields entirely and instead emit a single `request` field containing the raw request-line string (with escape sequences resolved per below).
- `status` (integer)
- `bytes_sent` (integer, or null when the source token is `-`; note: `%b`/`%B` of `-` becomes `null`, NOT `0`).
- For Combined Log Format additionally: `referer` (string or null), `user_agent` (string or null).

The `-` placeholder used in CLF/Combined for any missing field becomes JSON `null` (not the string `"-"`). Inside quoted fields (`%r`, `%{...}i`, referer, user-agent), Apache's backslash escapes — `\"`, `\\`, `\t`, `\n` — must be UNESCAPED in the emitted JSON string value (so an input token `"GET /a\"b HTTP/1.0"` yields `request_path` containing a literal `"`). A quoted field that contains an unterminated quote or a raw unescaped newline mid-quote is treated as a malformed line and produces an error record (see EDGE CASES).

CUSTOM FORMAT STRING. The tool must accept `--format` using Apache `LogFormat` directive syntax. Supported directives at minimum: `%h`, `%l`, `%u`, `%t`, `%r`, `%>s` and `%s`, `%b`, `%B`, `%D` (request duration in microseconds, integer field `duration_us`; when the source token is `-`, `duration_us` is `null`), and `%{Header}i`. `%{Header}i` directives are emitted under a top-level `headers` object whose key is the header name written in the format string PRESERVING the original case verbatim (no lowercasing, no canonicalization) — so `%{X-Custom}i` produces `headers["X-Custom"]`. The two exceptions are `%{Referer}i` and `%{User-Agent}i` (matched case-insensitively on the directive header name only — i.e. `%{referer}i` and `%{user-agent}i` are equivalent forms), which map to top-level `referer`/`user_agent` for compatibility with Combined and do NOT appear in `headers`. Unknown directives must cause the tool to exit non-zero at startup with a message naming the directive — do not silently capture them.

EDGE CASES AND EXIT CODES.
- Empty input: zero output lines, exit code 0.
- A line that does not match the active format must NOT crash the tool. Emit a JSON record on stdout (not stderr) with exactly two fields: `error` (string describing the failure) and `raw` (original line with trailing `\n` and optional preceding `\r` stripped — i.e. both LF and CRLF endings handled — no other transformation). Continue processing subsequent lines.
- Exit code is 0 whenever the tool ran to completion over its input — INCLUDING the case where every line was malformed and every output record carried an `error` field. Exit code 1 only for startup failures: invalid `--format` directive, unknown directive, file path not readable.
- The tool must stream: it reads and emits line-by-line and its resident memory must not grow with input size. The judge applies a generous, language-agnostic relative-growth bound (see test procedure); the intent is to forbid slurp-the-whole-file patterns like `readlines()`, not to demand micro-optimization. Memory should be O(1) in input length.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

FIXTURE SOURCE. The judge hand-authors ~50 log lines from the Apache HTTP Server access-log docs (https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/logs.html) and the `mod_log_config` reference (https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_log_config.html). Coverage:
- Canonical CLF and Combined examples verbatim from the Apache docs (auto-detect mode).
- Lines with `-` placeholders in each of `remote_logname`, `remote_user`, `bytes_sent`, `referer`, `user_agent`.
- Lines with IPv4, IPv6 (`::1`, `2001:db8::1`), and hostname forms of `%h`.
- Timestamps with non-UTC offsets: `-0700`, `+0530`, `+0000`.
- Quoted fields containing embedded spaces and backslash-escaped quotes (`\"`) inside `%r` and `%{User-Agent}i`.
- A malformed/truncated request line that cannot split into method/path/protocol (well-formed line overall, but `%r` has !=3 tokens) — exercises the `request` fallback field.
- A line with an unterminated quote in `%r` (truly malformed) — exercises the error-record path.
- A custom `--format` invocation exercising `%D` (both a real microsecond value and a `-` token) and a non-Referer/non-User-Agent `%{X-Custom}i` header (to verify the `headers` object mapping with case preserved).
- A custom `--format` invocation using `%{user-agent}i` (lowercase) to verify it still maps to top-level `user_agent`.
- An `--format` invocation with an unknown directive (must exit non-zero, stderr names the directive).
- An invocation with `--format` and the FILE argument in reversed order to verify argument-order independence.
- Empty stdin and a synthetic ~100k-line input piped via a named FIFO (to verify true streaming, not slurp).
- A CRLF-terminated malformed line to verify `raw` in the error record has both `\r` and `\n` stripped.

PASS CRITERIA (all must hold).
1. Field equivalence: for each well-formed fixture, the emitted JSON object must contain the expected fields with the expected values. The judge compares by parsing both sides as JSON and checking key/value equality for the normative fields: `remote_host`, `remote_logname`, `remote_user`, `status`, `bytes_sent`, `request_method`, `request_path`, `request_protocol` (or `request` for the unsplittable case — the three split fields must then be ABSENT, not null), `referer`, `user_agent`, `duration_us` where applicable (including `null` when source token is `-`), and `headers["X-Custom"]` (exact case) for the custom-header case. Extra fields not in this list are ignored.
2. Timestamp: the emitted `timestamp` value, when parsed as ISO-8601, must yield the same instant as the source Apache timestamp, AND the trailing offset substring must be a colon-separated form: `-07:00`, `+05:30`, or for a source offset of `+0000` either `+00:00` or `Z`. A timestamp lacking the colon (e.g. `-0700`) or normalized to UTC without preserving a non-zero source offset fails.
3. `bytes_sent` for `-` input is JSON `null` literal; `0` fails this probe. Likewise `duration_us` for `-` input is `null`.
4. Backslash-escaped quotes inside quoted fields are unescaped in the JSON value (the JSON string contains a literal `"`).
5. Header key casing: `headers["X-Custom"]` is present with exactly that key; `headers["x-custom"]` or any lowercased variant fails. The `%{user-agent}i` lowercase fixture still produces a top-level `user_agent` field.
6. At least 95% of the well-formed fixtures must pass (1)-(5). 100% of malformed fixtures (unterminated-quote line and any line failing both Combined and CLF) must produce a stdout record with an `error` field and a `raw` field equal to the input line with line endings (LF or CRLF) stripped, without process crash.
7. Empty input: zero stdout bytes, exit code 0.
8. All-malformed input: process exits 0 (every line yielded an `error` record).
9. Unknown directive in `--format`: process exits non-zero and stderr names the offending directive.
10. Argument-order independence: `logparse FILE --format FMT` and `logparse --format FMT FILE` produce byte-identical stdout.
11. Streaming check via FIFO: the judge writes 100k lines to a named pipe consumed by the tool, sampling RSS at 1 Hz. RSS measured at end-of-stream must not exceed RSS measured after the first 1k lines by more than 50 MB (generous, language-agnostic relative-growth bound). Wall-clock must complete within 120 s on the judge machine. There is NO absolute RSS ceiling.

Oracle tier rationale: Apache LogFormat is a stable external spec with normative documentation, but no machine-readable conformance suite exists, so the judge hand-authors fixtures from the spec (tier2). If the implementor ships repo tests covering the same behavior, the judge runs them as a secondary signal only.

---


## #32 — `simple-templating`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=3.0 PS=2.0 LP=5.0 | T=5.0 Or=4.0 DC=5.0 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=4.0 | Quality=4.5

**Oracle:** tier2-external-spec — https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/templates/

**Artifact shape:** CLI binary that reads a template file and a JSON context file, prints rendered output to stdout

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a small templating engine as a standalone CLI tool. The CLI must accept two positional arguments in this order: a path to a template file, then a path to a JSON file containing the rendering context. Invocation is `render <template_path> <context_json_path>`. Rendered output is written to stdout. On success the process exits with code 0. On error (file not found, malformed JSON, template syntax errors), the tool must exit non-zero and write a message to stderr. The error message MUST contain one of the category keywords `syntax`, `IO`, or `JSON` (case-insensitive) so a caller can classify what went wrong. (An approximate source location for syntax errors is encouraged for usability but is NOT checked by the test harness.)

The template language is a Jinja2 subset. Implementors should treat Jinja2's default behavior — configured as `autoescape=True`, default `Undefined`, `keep_trailing_newline=True`, default `trim_blocks=False`, default `lstrip_blocks=False`, with a single user filter `raw` aliased to `safe` — as the reference oracle for any ambiguity. The language and semantics below are pinned to match that configuration.

Required features:
1. Variable interpolation `{{ expr }}` where `expr` is one of:
   - A name (e.g. `user`).
   - A dotted attribute/key path (e.g. `user.name`).
   - A numeric segment in a dotted path is an array index (e.g. `items.0.title`). Bracket syntax `items[0].title` is NOT required and will not be probed.
   - A literal: integer, float, string (single or double quoted), `true`, `false`, `null`. Jinja's `none`/`True`/`False` spellings are NOT required; the JSON spellings are sufficient. Literal interpolations DO appear in fixtures (e.g. `{{ "a&b" }}`) and follow the same HTML-escaping rule as any other interpolation unless `|raw` is applied.
   - A pipe-applied filter; only the `raw` filter is required (alias for Jinja's `|safe`, suppressing HTML escaping for that one interpolation). No other filters will be probed.
2. Conditional blocks `{% if expr %} ... {% elif expr %} ... {% else %} ... {% endif %}` with arbitrary nesting. Inside `if`/`elif` expressions, support:
   - Comparison operators `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`.
   - Boolean operators `and`, `or`, `not`.
   - String literals on either side of a comparison (e.g. `{% if role == "admin" %}`).
   - Numeric, boolean, and null literals.
3. Loop blocks `{% for item in collection %} ... {% endfor %}`:
   - Iteration over a JSON array yields its elements in order.
   - Iteration over a JSON object yields its KEYS in JSON insertion order (matching Jinja's default `for x in dict`). The two-name `for k, v in dict.items()` form is NOT required and will not be probed.
   - The loop variable is lexically scoped to the block; inner loops shadow outer bindings; bindings do not leak outside the `{% endfor %}`.
   - Jinja's special `loop` variable (`loop.index`, `loop.first`, etc.) is NOT required and will not be probed.
   - Iterating an empty collection produces no output and no error.
4. Comments `{# ... #}` are stripped from output. Comments may span multiple lines. Nested comments are NOT required and will not be probed. The comment tag itself (including its delimiters) is removed; literal text on either side is preserved verbatim.
5. Literal text outside `{{ }}` / `{% %}` / `{# #}` is emitted verbatim with no escaping of that literal text.

Value-to-string rendering for interpolations (this matches Python/Jinja's `str()`):
- JSON string → its characters (then HTML-escaped unless `|raw`).
- Integer → decimal representation (e.g. `42`).
- Float → Python's `str(float)` representation (e.g. `3.14`, `1.0`). Fixtures will only use floats with exact short decimal representations (e.g. `0.5`, `1.5`, `2.0`) to avoid platform float-formatting drift.
- `true` → `True`, `false` → `False`, `null` → `None` (Jinja default).
- Arrays and objects interpolated directly: OUT OF SCOPE. No fixture interpolates a raw array or object value; only their scalar elements/fields are interpolated.

Truthiness (for `if`/`elif` and for `and`/`or`/`not`) follows Jinja/Python: `false`, `null`, `0`, `0.0`, empty string `""`, empty array `[]`, and empty object `{}` are falsy; everything else is truthy.

HTML escaping is ON by default for ALL interpolated values, including string literals inside `{{ }}`. The required character→entity mapping matches `markupsafe.escape` exactly:
- `&` → `&amp;`
- `<` → `&lt;`
- `>` → `&gt;`
- `"` → `&#34;`
- `'` → `&#39;`

Emit these exact entity forms (the numeric forms `&#34;` and `&#39;`, not `&quot;` / `&apos;`) so output is byte-deterministic. The `|raw` filter disables escaping for that one interpolation.

Undefined / missing-path semantics: An interpolation `{{ x }}` where the name or any segment of a dotted path is not present in the context renders as the empty string. This also applies when an intermediate segment exists but is a scalar (e.g. `user.name.first` where `user.name` is a string): traversal into a non-container yields the empty string, no error. No "undefined" sentinel is printed and no error is raised.

Whitespace: Jinja's `-` trim modifiers (`{%-`, `-%}`, `{{-`, `-}}`) are NOT required and will not appear in fixtures. Because the oracle uses `trim_blocks=False` and `lstrip_blocks=False` (Jinja defaults), the engine MUST preserve all whitespace and newlines around `{% %}`, `{{ }}`, and `{# #}` tags verbatim — including a newline immediately following a closing `%}` or `#}`. Do not implement any automatic newline-stripping after block tags. The oracle also uses `keep_trailing_newline=True`, so a trailing newline in the template file MUST be preserved in the output. Tests compare byte-for-byte.

Edge cases that must be handled:
- Empty template file → empty output, exit 0.
- Template with no tags → passthrough verbatim, exit 0.
- Template that is only a comment → empty output (modulo any literal whitespace around the comment), exit 0.
- Unterminated `{% if %}` or `{% for %}`, mismatched end tags (`{% endfor %}` closing an `{% if %}`), or other malformed tags → exit non-zero with stderr containing `syntax` (case-insensitive).
- Missing template or context file → exit non-zero with stderr containing `IO` (case-insensitive).
- Malformed JSON context → exit non-zero with stderr containing `JSON` (case-insensitive).
- Nested loops/conditionals to arbitrary depth.

Implementation language is the implementor's choice. The tool must be invocable as `render <tpl> <ctx.json>` from the repo root (a shell wrapper at `./render`, a Python entry point on PATH, or a compiled binary are all fine). Include a README or `--help` documenting invocation.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

The judge runs the candidate's `render` binary against a held-out fixture suite of 30-50 (template, context.json, expected_output) triples. The oracle is the Jinja2 Python library (latest stable on PyPI) configured exactly as:

```
env = jinja2.Environment(
    autoescape=True,
    undefined=jinja2.Undefined,        # default; renders missing as ''
    keep_trailing_newline=True,
    trim_blocks=False,                  # default
    lstrip_blocks=False,                # default
)
env.filters['raw'] = lambda x: jinja2.Markup(x)  # alias |raw to |safe
```

For each positive fixture, expected output is `env.from_string(open(tpl).read()).render(**json.load(open(ctx)))`.

Positive fixture categories (each contributes multiple fixtures):
(a) plain literal passthrough including multi-line text with no tags;
(b) variable interpolation: single names, dotted paths, numeric-segment array indexing (`items.0.title`, `matrix.1.2`), and scalar value-to-string rendering for integers, floats with short decimal reps, booleans (rendered as `True`/`False`), and null (rendered as `None`);
(c) HTML escaping of `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'` in interpolated values (including string-literal interpolations like `{{ "a&b" }}`); verification that `|raw` suppresses escaping; verification that literal `<`/`>`/`&` in template TEXT (outside `{{ }}`) is NOT escaped;
(d) `if`/`elif`/`else`/`endif` with truthy/falsy values across strings, numbers, empty containers, and null; comparison and boolean operators including string-literal comparisons (`x == "admin"`);
(e) `for` over arrays (including arrays of objects), iteration over a JSON object yielding its keys in insertion order, empty-array iteration producing empty output, nested loops with shadowing, scope leakage check (variable defined inside loop not visible after);
(f) `{# comment #}` removal in isolation, interleaved with text and tags, and at least one multi-line comment;
(g) deeply nested mixed blocks (>= 3 levels);
(h) empty template file; template that is only a comment; template that ends with a trailing newline (verifying `keep_trailing_newline` parity);
(i) undefined-variable interpolation rendering as empty string, including (i-1) a missing top-level name, (i-2) a dotted path with a missing intermediate segment, and (i-3) a dotted path that traverses into a scalar (e.g. `user.name.first` where `user.name` is a string).

Negative fixtures (exactly 4):
- N1: Template with unterminated `{% if x %}` (no `{% endif %}`).
- N2: Template with `{% if x %}...{% endfor %}` (mismatched end tag).
- N3: Context file that is not valid JSON.
- N4: Context file path that does not exist.

For each negative fixture: the candidate must exit non-zero, write non-empty stderr, and stderr must contain the appropriate category keyword (case-insensitive): `syntax` for N1/N2, `JSON` for N3, `IO` for N4. The presence or format of any line-number / location info is NOT checked.

Fixtures will NOT exercise: `loop.*`, `dict.items()`, bracket index syntax, `-` trim modifiers, filters other than `|raw`, direct interpolation of an array or object value, nested `{# #}` comments, or floats requiring non-trivial repr (e.g. `0.1 + 0.2`).

Comparison method for positive fixtures: strict byte-for-byte equality between candidate stdout and Jinja2's output.

Each fixture run has a 5-second wall-clock timeout; timeouts count as failures.

Pass criterion (all must hold):
1. At least 90% of positive fixtures match byte-for-byte.
2. Every fixture in category (c) (HTML escaping, including literal interpolations and the literal-text non-escaping check) and category (i) (undefined / missing-path / traverse-into-scalar) passes byte-for-byte — these are pinned, no partial credit.
3. All 4 negative fixtures produce non-zero exit, non-empty stderr, and the required category keyword.
4. No fixture run exceeds the 5-second timeout.

The fixture suite, oracle script, and harness are held out from the implementor.

---


## #33 — `ini-toml-converter`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=3.0 PS=2.0 LP=5.0 | T=4.0 Or=4.0 DC=5.0 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=4.0 | Quality=4.4

**Oracle:** tier2-external-spec — https://github.com/toml-lang/toml-test at tag v1.5.0 (TOML conformance suite; specifically `tests/valid/string/`, `tests/valid/integer/`, `tests/valid/float/`, `tests/valid/bool/`, `tests/valid/key/` 

**Artifact shape:** CLI tool (two subcommands of a single `initoml` binary, or two binaries `ini2toml` and `toml2ini`) reading from stdin or a file path argument, writing to stdout, with exit codes, `--help`, and stderr per spec.

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a command-line tool that converts between INI configuration files and TOML 1.0.0 configuration files. Expose two operations: `ini2toml` (reads an INI document from a file path argument or, if no path is given, from stdin; writes a TOML document to stdout) and `toml2ini` (reads TOML similarly; writes INI to stdout). These may be two subcommands of a single binary or two separate executables. You may use existing TOML parser/emitter libraries; INI handling you may write yourself.

Exit semantics: exit 0 on success with empty stderr. On malformed input, exit non-zero and write a single-line error message to stderr that contains (a) a 1-based decimal line number referring to the source file (matching the regex `\b\d+\b`; off-by-one is tolerated by the judge, so naive line tracking is fine) and (b) a short cause phrase. The error message must NOT contain a language-native stack trace: specifically it must not contain any of `Traceback`, `panicked at`, `goroutine`, a Java-style `\tat ` frame, or a JS/Node `\n  at ` frame. `--help` on each subcommand/binary must print a short usage message and exit 0.

INI dialect: Python `configparser` / "Windows INI" conventions — `[section]` headers, `key = value` or `key: value` pairs, `#` and `;` line comments, an implicit default/global section for keys before any header, case-sensitive keys preserved as written. Whitespace handling: leading and trailing whitespace around both keys and values is stripped (matches `configparser`). Empty keys, whitespace-only keys, and section names containing `]` are malformed input. Duplicate sections: merged (later keys append/overwrite). Duplicate keys within a section: last-wins (no error). A UTF-8 BOM at the start of the input is accepted and silently stripped.

`ini2toml` emission rules (MANDATORY for deterministic judging):
- Each INI section becomes a top-level TOML table. The implicit/global section's keys are emitted at the document root, before any `[table]` header.
- All values are emitted as TOML strings. Do NOT auto-infer integers/floats/bools/datetimes.
- Keys are emitted as TOML bare keys when they fully match `[A-Za-z0-9_-]+`, otherwise as quoted keys `"..."` with standard TOML basic-string escaping.
- Values containing an embedded newline (`\n`) are emitted using TOML multi-line basic strings (`"""..."""`); other values use single-line basic strings `"..."` with standard escaping.
- Output ordering MUST be deterministic. Required ordering: sections appear in first-appearance order from the input (the implicit/global section, if any, always first); keys within each section are emitted in sorted (lexicographic by Unicode codepoint) order. This deterministic ordering is mandatory, not optional, because round-trip byte-stability depends on it.
- Stderr must be empty on success.

`toml2ini` emission rules:
- Nested tables and dotted keys are flattened into INI section names using `.` as the separator (e.g. TOML `[a.b.c]` becomes INI `[a.b.c]`). This is fixed.
- TOML strings, integers, floats, booleans, and datetimes are stringified using their TOML literal form. For datetimes and floats, re-serialization via the chosen TOML library's canonical form is acceptable (no requirement to byte-preserve the source spelling). Booleans must be `true`/`false`; integers as decimal; floats including a decimal point or exponent.
- TOML string values containing literal newline characters cannot be losslessly represented in single-line INI. Implementations MUST escape such characters as `\n` (and `\\` for backslash, `\"` for double quote when emitted within a quoted value) on the INI side. To preserve round-trip stability with `ini2toml`, the round-trip claim (below) is RESTRICTED to TOML inputs whose string values contain no literal newline characters; values with embedded newlines round-trip through `ini2toml`'s `"""..."""` form but `toml2ini` of a newline-bearing TOML string is allowed to emit an escaped single-line form, which `ini2toml` would then read back as a literal string. Implementors do not need to handle this case losslessly.
- TOML arrays, inline tables, and arrays-of-tables that cannot be represented in INI must cause a non-zero exit with a line-numbered error message.
- TOML duplicate keys are a spec violation; surface them as the malformed-input error path. If the underlying TOML library does not expose line position for the duplicate, a line number of `1` is acceptable.

Round-trip byte-stability (MANDATORY): for any input on which neither direction errors, two consecutive passes of the same pipeline must produce byte-identical output. Concretely: `P1 = ini2toml(input)` and `P2 = ini2toml(toml2ini(P1))` must be byte-equal. The first pass is permitted to normalize whitespace, key ordering, and quoting style (per the deterministic-ordering rule above); the second pass must not change anything further. The analogous byte-equal fixed-point holds for `toml2ini | ini2toml` starting from TOML input restricted to the lossless subset (flat tables of scalar values with no embedded newlines in strings).

Performance: a 1 MB input containing ~10,000 key/value pairs must complete in well under 10 seconds on a single modern CPU core. Avoid O(n^2) constructions.

Edge cases: empty input (empty output, exit 0); comments-only / whitespace-only input; implicit default section; values containing `=`, single and double quotes, embedded newlines; UTF-8 in keys and values including BOM; surface malformed inputs as errors per the error semantics above.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

The judge evaluates with five oracles. All fixtures use UTF-8 input.

(1) INI->TOML emission conformance. The judge feeds ~15 hand-written INI fixtures through `ini2toml`. Each emitted TOML document is parsed with Python's stdlib `tomllib` (Python 3.11+). Pass requires: (a) parse succeeds with no exception; (b) the resulting Python dict is `==`-equal to the judge's expected dict, where every leaf value is a Python `str`. Before comparison, the judge strips leading/trailing whitespace from expected values (matching the spec's configparser-style whitespace handling). Stderr must be empty (zero bytes). Fixtures cover: single section with three keys; implicit default section; two sections; key containing a dot (must be quoted); value containing `=`; value containing a double quote; value containing an embedded newline (emitted via `"""..."""`); UTF-8 value with Greek and CJK; empty file; comments-only file; INI with duplicate key (last-wins); INI with same section header twice (merged); INI with leading UTF-8 BOM (accepted, stripped); INI with surrounding whitespace on values (stripped).

(2) TOML->INI->TOML round-trip on the toml-test lossless subset. The judge pins toml-test at tag `v1.5.0` and selects valid fixtures from `tests/valid/string/`, `tests/valid/integer/`, `tests/valid/float/`, `tests/valid/bool/`, and `tests/valid/key/` that consist of a single flat table (no nested tables, no arrays-of-tables, no inline tables, no arrays, and no string values containing literal newlines). Each fixture is run as `toml2ini < input.toml | ini2toml`, then parsed with `tomllib`. Comparison: the original TOML is also parsed with `tomllib` to produce a reference dict; every scalar leaf is normalized to its TOML-canonical string form (booleans as `true`/`false`; integers as Python `str(int)`; floats via Python `repr` rounded to a canonical form OR by re-emitting through the implementor's TOML library — the judge accepts either canonical form; datetimes via the TOML library's canonical RFC 3339 serialization). The round-trip dict's leaves (already strings) are compared to these normalized references with `==`. At least 90% of fixtures in the selected subset must pass. Stderr must be empty on successful runs. Fixtures outside the subset are run only to confirm `toml2ini` either errors cleanly (non-zero exit, no traceback) or round-trips per documented behavior; no positive correctness claim is required.

(3) Round-trip byte-stability. The judge runs ~30 generated INI fixtures (empty, comments-only, implicit-default-section, multi-section, quoted values, embedded `=`, UTF-8, BOM, duplicate keys, duplicate sections, values with embedded newlines, values with leading/trailing whitespace) through two passes: `P1 = ini2toml(input)`; `P2 = ini2toml(toml2ini(P1))`. Pass requires `P1 == P2` byte-for-byte for every fixture where neither pass errors. Stderr must be empty on both passes. No structural-only fallback.

(4) Malformed-input handling. The judge feeds: an INI header missing `]`; an INI key with no `=` or `:` separator on a non-comment, non-blank line that is not a section header; a TOML file with an unterminated basic string; a TOML file with a duplicate key; a TOML file with a key with no value. For each, pass requires (a) non-zero exit code; (b) stderr contains a decimal integer matching `\b\d+\b` whose value equals the line where the error occurs, plus or minus one (off-by-one tolerated); (c) stderr does NOT contain any of the substrings `Traceback`, `panicked at`, `goroutine`, `\tat ` (Java/Kotlin stack frame), or `\n  at ` (JS/Node stack frame); (d) stderr is a single line (no embedded newline except possibly a single trailing newline).

(5) Performance. The judge generates a 1 MB INI file with 10,000 unique `key_N = value_N` pairs across 100 sections and runs `ini2toml` on it. Pass requires wall-clock completion in under 10 seconds on a single modern x86_64 core. Stderr empty on success.

`--help`: the judge runs `--help` on each entry point and requires exit code 0 with no crash; content is not inspected.

Aggregate pass criteria: (a) 100% of oracle (1) fixtures pass; (b) >=90% of the selected toml-test subset round-trips in oracle (2); (c) 100% of oracle (3) fixtures reach byte-equal fixed point; (d) 100% of oracle (4) malformed cases exit non-zero with line-numbered, traceback-free, single-line stderr; (e) oracle (5) completes within the time bound; (f) `--help` exits 0 on every entry point.

---


## #34 — `json5-parser`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=3.0 PS=3.0 LP=5.0 | T=5.0 Or=5.0 DC=5.0 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=5.0 | Quality=5.0

**Oracle:** tier1-external-suite — https://github.com/json5/json5-tests

**Artifact shape:** CLI binary that reads JSON5 from stdin (or a file argument) and writes canonical JSON to stdout; non-zero exit on parse error with a structured error message on stderr containing a line and column reference.

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a command-line tool invocable as `json5parse` (either a binary or an executable wrapper script with that exact filename on PATH or in the project root) that reads JSON5 text and emits canonical JSON. The tool accepts input either from a single positional file argument or, if none is provided, from standard input. The literal argument `-` means "read from stdin." On a successful parse, it writes a valid JSON document to standard output and exits with status 0. On a parse error it must (a) write NOTHING to standard output, (b) write a non-empty error message to standard error that contains, at minimum, the substring `line` followed (with any non-digit run between them) by a 1-indexed integer >= 1, and the substring `column` followed similarly by a 1-indexed integer >= 1, plus a short human-readable description of the problem; the stderr message MAY be multi-line and MAY be JSON, key=value, or prose, as long as the regexes `line[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` and `column[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` both match with captured values >= 1, and (c) exit with a non-zero status. When possible, the reported position should point at the offending token (e.g., the opening quote of an unterminated string, the first bareword character of `{a: foo}`) rather than at EOF. Malformed input must never crash the process: no segfault, no uncaught language-level exception leaking a stack trace to the terminal, no hang beyond a few seconds.

Implementation constraints: pick one language from {Python, JavaScript/Node, TypeScript, Go, Rust, Java, C/C++}. The artifact must be runnable as a standalone command after a documented build step so that `./json5parse < file.json5` works from a clean checkout (a shell wrapper invoking an interpreter is fine). You MUST NOT use a third-party JSON5 parser (e.g., the `json5` npm package, Python `pyjson5`/`json5`, Go `github.com/titanous/json5`, etc.); the JSON5 grammar must be implemented by hand. Standard-library JSON serialization for the OUTPUT side is explicitly allowed and encouraged. Standard-library Unicode-category lookups (e.g., Python `unicodedata`, ICU, Go `unicode`) are allowed.

The parser must implement the full JSON5 grammar as published at https://spec.json5.org/ — treat that spec as the ground truth, and the bullets below as a checklist of features that are commonly missed rather than a substitute for reading the spec:
- Object keys may be double-quoted strings, single-quoted strings, or ECMAScript 5.1 IdentifierNames (Unicode `ID_Start` / `ID_Continue` plus `$` and `_`, with `\uXXXX` escapes permitted inside identifiers).
- Trailing commas in objects and arrays are allowed.
- Single-quoted strings and multi-line strings via backslash-newline line continuations are allowed.
- String escapes: `\'`, `\"`, `\\`, `\b`, `\f`, `\n`, `\r`, `\t`, `\v`, `\0` (only when the next character is not a decimal digit; `\0` at end-of-input is allowed and decodes to U+0000), `\xNN`, `\uNNNN`, and the "escape any other non-escape character" rule (e.g., `\q` decodes to `q`; exceptions: line terminators after `\` form a line continuation, and a digit after `\0` is forbidden, and digits 1-9 after `\` are forbidden).
- Numbers: leading/trailing decimal points (`.5`, `5.`), explicit `+` sign, hexadecimal literals (`0x...`/`0X...`), and the special tokens `Infinity`, `-Infinity`, `+Infinity`, `NaN`, `-NaN`, `+NaN`.
- Single-line (`//`) and multi-line (`/* */`) comments anywhere whitespace is allowed.
- The JSON5 whitespace set: space, tab, vertical tab, form feed, LF, CR, LS U+2028, PS U+2029, NBSP U+00A0, BOM U+FEFF, and any character in the Unicode `Zs` category.
- A UTF-8 BOM at the start of input is silently accepted. Whitespace and comments before and after the top-level value are allowed.

Rejection rules:
- Inputs that are valid ECMAScript object literals but not valid JSON5 must be rejected — e.g., bareword values like `{a: foo}`, function literals, or computed properties.
- Empty input and input containing only whitespace/comments are parse errors (JSON5 requires exactly one value).
- After the top-level value, only whitespace and comments may follow before EOF; any other trailing content (a second value, stray punctuation, an unterminated comment) is a parse error.
- Anything else not derivable from the JSON5 grammar at https://spec.json5.org/ is a parse error.

Line and column accounting (used only for error reporting; the judge does not check exact values, only that integers >= 1 are present, but the implementor should not have to guess):
- "Column" is a 1-indexed count of Unicode codepoints since the last line break on the current line. Tabs count as one column. Surrogate pairs in input are not expected (input is UTF-8) but if encountered count as one codepoint each.
- "Line" is 1-indexed and increments on any of: LF (`\n`), CR (`\r`) not followed by LF, CRLF (counts as one increment), LS (U+2028), or PS (U+2029).
- The numeric value of line/column at any given parse error is not graded; only the presence of `line N` and `column M` with N,M >= 1 is required.

Numeric semantics: numbers are parsed into IEEE 754 doubles. The semantic-equivalence oracle compares with float tolerance, so integer precision beyond 2^53 is not required. Numbers that overflow IEEE 754 (e.g., `1e9999`) map to `Infinity`/`-Infinity`; explicit `Infinity`, `-Infinity`, `+Infinity`, and `NaN` tokens are accepted as input. ALL non-finite values (overflow-produced or explicit) MUST be emitted as JSON `null` in the output, matching `JSON.stringify`. Never emit the bareword `Infinity` or `NaN` in output JSON.

Canonical output format (RFC 8259):
- Double-quoted strings only; no trailing commas; no comments; identifier-style keys quoted.
- Hex literals and `+`-prefixed numbers normalized to plain decimal form. Numbers that are mathematically integers (e.g., parsed from `1.0`, `0x10`, `1e2`) SHOULD be emitted in integer form when representable as an exact integer; otherwise emit a standard decimal/`e`-notation form. The oracle compares numerically.
- Non-finite numbers are emitted as JSON `null` (see above).
- Non-ASCII characters in strings MAY be emitted as raw UTF-8 or as `\uXXXX` escapes — both accepted, since the oracle re-parses the output before comparing.
- Output is a single line terminated by exactly one `\n`, with `,` between members/elements and `:` between key and value, with no extra whitespace.
- Duplicate keys in input follow JavaScript object-property semantics: the parsed object behaves as a map keyed by string, so the final occurrence of each key is what is emitted.

Performance and robustness:
- The tool must handle inputs up to several megabytes efficiently.
- The tool must not stack-overflow on nested arrays/objects up to at least 5000 levels deep. Prefer an iterative parser, or raise the host language's recursion limit (e.g., `sys.setrecursionlimit` in Python, larger goroutine stacks in Go) as part of program startup.
- No input within a few megabytes should take more than a few seconds of wall-clock time on a modern laptop; the per-fixture hard cap enforced by the judge is 5 seconds.

Edge cases the implementor should think about: empty input, whitespace/comment-only input, deeply nested structures (>= 5000), repeated object keys, Unicode in strings and identifiers, very large and very small finite numbers, overflow to Infinity, explicit `Infinity`/`NaN` mapping to null, UTF-8 BOM at start of input, trailing garbage after the top-level value, and unterminated strings/comments. Malformed input must always produce a structured stderr error with `line N`/`column M` and a non-zero exit; never a crash and never any stdout output.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

PRIMARY ORACLE: the official JSON5 conformance test corpus at https://github.com/json5/json5-tests (pinned to the latest commit on `main` at judge time). The judge clones the repository and runs the candidate `json5parse` against every fixture in the corpus. Each fixture is classified by extension:
- `.json` and `.json5` files MUST parse successfully (exit 0, valid JSON on stdout, nothing on stderr).
- `.js` files (technically-valid-ECMAScript-but-not-JSON5) and `.txt` files MUST fail to parse (non-zero exit, EMPTY stdout, non-empty stderr containing the substrings `line` and `column` followed by integers >= 1).

A fixture is "classified correctly" iff exit status, stdout-emptiness on failure, and stderr-presence-with-line/column on failure all match the above contract. The judge computes classification pass-rate over the full corpus and requires >= 95% correct. Any crash (segfault, uncaught exception with stack trace on stderr, abort) or any hang exceeding 5 seconds wall-clock on a single fixture counts as an automatic fail for that fixture and a hard cap of 90% on the overall task score regardless of other results.

SEMANTIC EQUIVALENCE: for each `.json`/`.json5` success fixture, the judge parses the candidate's stdout with Python `json.loads` and compares structurally against the expected value obtained by running the reference `json5` npm package (https://www.npmjs.com/package/json5, latest release at judge time) on the same input and re-serializing with `JSON.stringify`. Comparison rules:
- Objects compared as unordered key->value maps (key order is irrelevant).
- Arrays compared positionally.
- Strings compared by codepoint sequence after JSON unescaping (so `\uXXXX` vs raw UTF-8 in candidate output does not matter).
- Numbers compared with `math.isclose(a, b, rel_tol=1e-12, abs_tol=1e-12)`; an expected `null` arising from an `Infinity`/`NaN` input matches a candidate `null`.
- Booleans and `null` compared by identity.
At least 95% of `.json`/`.json5` fixtures must produce semantically-equivalent output.

CURATED PROBE SET (held out from the implementor; all probes must pass with no crashes or hangs — 100% required):
1. Empty input (zero bytes) -> non-zero exit, empty stdout, stderr matching `line[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` and `column[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` with captures >= 1.
2. Whitespace-only input (`"   \n\t  "`) -> parse error per #1's contract.
3. Comment-only input (`"// hello\n/* world */"`) -> parse error per #1's contract.
4. UTF-8 BOM followed by `{"a":1}` -> exit 0, stdout semantically equals `{"a":1}`.
5. Duplicate-key input `{"a":1,"a":2}` -> exit 0, stdout semantically equals `{"a":2}`.
6. "Kitchen sink" input mixing trailing commas, single quotes, identifier keys, hex numbers, `Infinity`, `NaN`, line continuations, both comment styles, BOM, and Unicode identifiers -> exit 0, stdout semantically matches reference `json5` parse (with `Infinity`/`NaN` represented as `null` on both sides).
7. ~1 MB synthetic input of the form `[[[[ ... 0 ... ]]]]` with nesting depth 5000 -> exit 0 within 5 seconds, stdout semantically equals the same nested structure.
8. Unterminated string `"abc` -> non-zero exit, empty stdout, stderr containing `line` and `column` followed by integers (line must be `1`; column is any integer >= 1).
9. Valid-ECMAScript-but-not-JSON5 input `{a: foo}` (bareword value) -> non-zero exit, empty stdout, structured stderr per the regex contract.
10. Number overflow `1e9999` at top level -> exit 0, stdout semantically equals `null` (i.e., `null\n` after canonicalization).

For probes #1, #2, #3, #8, and #9, the judge verifies stderr by running the regexes `line[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` and `column[^0-9]*([0-9]+)` against the entire stderr buffer (which may be multi-line) and requires both captured integers to be >= 1.

The CLI is invoked as `./json5parse <fixture-path>` (file-argument form) for corpus runs and as `./json5parse < fixture` (stdin form) for at least one probe (#1, empty stdin) so both input paths are exercised.

PASS CRITERION (all must hold):
- >= 95% classification pass-rate on the json5-tests corpus.
- >= 95% semantic-equivalence pass-rate on success fixtures.
- 100% pass on the 10-probe curated set.
- Zero crashes and zero hangs (> 5 s) across the entire run.

TIE-BREAKING REFERENCES: https://spec.json5.org/ for grammar questions, RFC 8259 for canonical JSON output rules, and the behavior of the reference `json5` npm package for any remaining ambiguity.

IMPLEMENTATION-CHECK (post hoc, lower weight): the judge greps the candidate source for imports/requires of third-party JSON5 libraries (`json5`, `pyjson5`, `simplejson5`, `github.com/titanous/json5`, etc.). Use of such a library is grounds for marking the task failed regardless of corpus pass-rate, since it bypasses the actual exercise.

---


## #35 — `mini-language`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=3.7 PS=3.3 LP=5.0 | T=4.0 Or=2.0 DC=5.0 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=4.0 | Quality=4.1

**Oracle:** tier5-generated-tests — none

**Artifact shape:** CLI binary `mini` exposing subcommands `run <file>`, `repl`, and reading from stdin when no args; programs use `.mini` extension

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a small dynamically-typed programming language called "mini", delivered as a single CLI executable named `mini`. The CLI must support: `mini run path/to/file.mini` to execute a program file, `mini repl` to start an interactive read-eval-print loop, and reading a program from stdin when invoked as `mini -` or with no positional argument and stdin is not a TTY. Programs use the `.mini` file extension. The exit code must be 0 on successful execution and non-zero on parse errors, runtime errors, or uncaught exceptions. The program's normal output goes to stdout; errors go to stderr. For `mini run path/to/file.mini`, the file is loaded from the given path (resolved against the process cwd if relative), but module imports inside that file are always resolved relative to the importing file's own directory regardless of cwd.

ERROR FORMAT (required, concrete): every parse error and runtime error printed to stderr must include a location prefix of the form `<path>:<line>:<col>: <message>` where `<line>` and `<col>` are positive integers (1-based) and `<path>` is the source file path (or a stable placeholder like `<stdin>` or `<repl>` for non-file sources). The colon-separated `line:col` token must appear on the same line as the message. Example: `examples/bad.mini:3:5: unterminated string literal`.

LANGUAGE FEATURES: expression-oriented; literals for integer, float, string, boolean, nil, list, and map (dictionary); arithmetic (`+ - * / %`), comparison (`< <= > >= == !=`), and logical (`and or not`) operators with conventional precedence (multiplicative binds tighter than additive; comparison tighter than logical; unary minus); `let`/assignment for variables with lexical scoping; `if/else` conditional that yields a value usable in expression position (e.g., `let x = if cond then a else b` or the equivalent surface syntax the implementor chooses — the conditional must be usable as an rvalue); `while` and `for`-in loops; first-class functions with closures defined via a `fn` keyword (or equivalent) supporting recursion; and a `return` statement. `break` and `continue` are NOT required (loops may be exited via flags or early `return` from an enclosing function). Strings support escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`) and concatenation with `+`. Lists and maps support indexing (`xs[0]`, `m["key"]`) and assignment-to-index for BOTH lists (`xs[0] = v`) and maps (`m["k"] = v`). Map keys may be strings only (other key types are not required; the implementation may reject non-string keys at parse or runtime — this is not tested). The implementor chooses whether built-in operations (length, append, keys, etc.) are exposed as methods or stdlib functions and must document the choice in the README.

NUMERIC SEMANTICS (implementor's choice, MUST be documented in README): the implementor chooses (a) whether `/` between two integers yields an integer (truncating or flooring) or a float, and (b) whether `%` truncates or floors. The README must state the chosen rule. Generated tests will only assert exact-integer results where both operands and the chosen rule unambiguously produce an integer (e.g., `6 / 2`, `7 % 3` with non-negative operands), and float results where at least one operand is a float literal.

STANDARD LIBRARY (required, accessible without import): at minimum `print`, `println`, `len`, `str`, `int`, `float`, `type`, plus list/map helpers sufficient to obtain length, append to a list, and enumerate map keys. `print` writes its argument to stdout WITHOUT a trailing newline; `println` writes its argument followed by exactly one `\n`. When `print`/`println` are given a string value they emit the raw string contents (no surrounding quotes). Single-argument calls are sufficient for all required tests; multi-argument support is optional, and if supported the separator and zero-argument behavior must be documented — generated tests will only call `print`/`println` with exactly one argument. `len` on a string returns either byte count or codepoint count at the implementor's choice (MUST be documented in README); tests will only call `len` on ASCII strings where the two values coincide. Both stdout and stderr (in CLI and REPL) must be line-buffered or flushed after each top-level statement/result, so a harness reading via pipes observes output without waiting for process exit.

MODULES: a program may import another `.mini` file via `import "relative/path"` (or equivalent syntax documented in the README). The imported module's top-level bindings are exposed under a namespace whose name is the file's basename with the `.mini` extension stripped (e.g., `import "lib.mini"` or `import "lib"` yields namespace `lib`; non-identifier characters such as hyphens may be normalized to underscores — document the rule). Member access uses `module.name`. Imports are resolved relative to the importing file's directory (NOT the process cwd). Modules are executed at most once and the resulting namespace is cached; subsequent imports return the cached namespace. Circular imports must be detected and reported as a structured error (using the standard error format above) whose message clearly identifies the condition — the message must contain the literal word "circular" or "cycle" (case-insensitive) so the cause is unambiguous.

REPL: `mini repl` must work whether stdin is a TTY or a pipe (do NOT gate on isatty). Bindings persist across input lines. Bare expressions are evaluated and their result printed on its own line using a `str`-style representation (strings print without surrounding quotes; numbers print in their natural decimal form; nil prints as `nil`; booleans as `true`/`false`; lists/maps in a readable bracketed form — document the exact format in the README). If a printed value itself contains embedded newlines (e.g., a string with `\n`), the printed value occupies as many physical lines as needed; "on its own line" means it begins after a newline boundary and ends with a newline. Statements whose value is nil (such as `let x = 1`, assignments, `while` loops, calls to `println`) produce no extra REPL output beyond whatever the statement itself wrote to stdout. After a parse error or runtime error, the REPL must print the error to stderr and continue reading; the next valid expression's result must still be printed normally. stdout must be flushed after each result line so a pipe-driven harness sees output promptly, and the error path must not leave partial bytes in stdout that would corrupt subsequent result lines. No prompt prefix (like `>>>` or `=>`) is required on result lines.

EDGE CASES (required behaviors): empty input file exits 0 with no output; files containing only comments or whitespace likewise; division by zero is a structured runtime error; out-of-bounds list indexing is a structured runtime error; calling a non-function value is a structured runtime error; reading an undefined variable is a structured error with location at the use site; unterminated string literals and unmatched brackets are structured parse errors with location; the interpreter must handle recursion depth of at least 200 frames on reasonable inputs without crashing (the generated test exercises a recursive fibonacci(n=20) and a recursive factorial(n=12), so a usable native call stack of a few hundred frames is sufficient); ASCII identifiers are required and UTF-8 bytes inside string literals must pass through unchanged (non-ASCII identifiers are optional).

NON-FUNCTIONAL: the interpreter should run small recursive and iterative workloads in interactive time (a few-thousand-iteration `while` loop should complete in a couple of seconds on a developer laptop). Ship a README describing: the grammar (or a sketch), the exact built-in names and whether they are functions or methods, the import syntax and namespace-naming rule, the REPL result-printing format, the numeric-division rule, and the `len`-on-string rule, and how to invoke the CLI.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

The artifact is judged via a black-box test harness invoking the `mini` binary on a corpus of small `.mini` programs and comparing stdout, stderr, and exit code against expected values. The harness is generated by the judge after reading the artifact's README (to learn the implementor's chosen built-in surface, import syntax, numeric-division rule, and `len`-on-string rule), so probes adapt to the documented API while testing the required behaviors. The harness uses subprocess invocation with a 10-second per-program timeout and lives at a held-out path. Tests are tier5 generated. Concrete numeric examples (operand values, expected results) shown below are illustrative — the held-out fixtures will use different but analogous inputs.

PROBE SET (each probe is one or more fixtures):
1. Arithmetic + precedence: integer and float arithmetic exercising mixed precedence (e.g., `a + b * c` and `(a + b) * c`), unary minus, integer/float mixing, modulo on non-negative operands, and integer division on operands whose result is unambiguous under either truncation or floor semantics (e.g., evenly divisible non-negative integers, or where the README documents float division). Compared exactly on stdout. Fixtures that would depend on the chosen `/` or `%` rule for negative operands are not used.
2. Strings: concatenation with `+`, escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`), `len` on an ASCII-only string (byte count and codepoint count coincide, so either documented choice passes).
3. Collections: list construction, indexing, mutation via index assignment (`xs[0] = v`), iteration via `for`-in; map construction with string keys, indexing by string key, mutation via map index assignment (`m["k"] = v`), iteration over keys.
4. Closures + recursion: a counter factory (closure capturing an outer `let`) called multiple times produces incrementing values; recursive `factorial(12)` and recursive `fibonacci(20)` produce exact expected results. (These exercise <= ~30 native frames; well within the required 200-frame depth.)
5. Control flow: `if/else` used as an expression in rvalue position (e.g., assigned to a `let` and the bound name printed) returning the correct branch value; `while` loop exited via a boolean flag (no `break` required).
6. Module import (happy path): two-file program where `main.mini` does `import "lib.mini"` (or the documented equivalent) and calls `lib.<fn>(...)`; the harness invokes `mini run` from a working directory DIFFERENT from the directory containing `main.mini` to verify import resolution uses the importing file's directory, not cwd.
7. Module caching: importing the same module twice executes its top level once (verified by a side-effecting `println` at module top level appearing exactly once in stdout).
8. Circular import: two files importing each other must exit non-zero AND emit a stderr line matching the error format `<path>:<line>:<col>: <msg>` whose message portion contains the literal substring `circular` or `cycle` (case-insensitive).
9. REPL recovery: `mini repl` is driven via piped stdin containing (a) a valid `let` binding, (b) a deliberate parse error line, (c) a valid expression that uses the binding from (a). The harness asserts that the result of (c) appears on stdout (matched as a substring on its own line — i.e., bounded by newline characters or stream start/end), the process exits 0 at EOF, and at least one stderr line was produced for (b). Result formatting follows the README's documented rule. The harness reads stdout and stderr on separate file descriptors and does NOT require them to be interleaved in any particular order; it only requires that the result of (c) on stdout is not corrupted by error bytes.
10. Error edge cases: empty file (exit 0, empty stdout, empty stderr); whitespace-only file (same); division by zero; out-of-bounds list index; undefined variable; unterminated string literal; unmatched bracket. Each non-empty case must exit non-zero and produce at least one stderr line matching the regex `[^:\s]+:\d+:\d+:` (the documented `path:line:col:` prefix).

MATCHING RULES:
- stdout for probes 1-7 is compared by exact string equality (trailing newline tolerated).
- stderr is matched by regex/substring as specified per probe; exact stderr equality is never required.
- Exit codes are compared as a class: 0 vs non-zero (any non-zero is acceptable for failure cases).
- For REPL result lines, the harness uses substring match on the documented representation read from the README, bounded by newline characters so a line containing the substring as part of an unrelated value does not falsely match.
- All `print`/`println` invocations in fixtures use exactly one argument, so multi-arg formatting choices are not tested.

PASS CRITERION: at least 80% of generated fixtures pass. The following subsets must pass at 100% or the artifact fails overall regardless of total: probe 1 (arithmetic), probe 4 (closures + recursion), probe 6 (basic import), probe 9 (REPL recovery), and the empty-file case from probe 10. Probe 8 (circular import) and the `path:line:col` regex check in probe 10 must each pass on at least one fixture.

NON-FUNCTIONAL CHECKS (informational, not part of the 80% but counted as fixtures with their own pass/fail): a `while` loop summing 1..3000 completes within 2 seconds; recursive fibonacci(20) completes within the 10-second subprocess timeout. The fibonacci check is treated as a smoke test for recursion correctness rather than a perf bar.

README CHECK: the judge does a quick manual read of the README to confirm the built-ins, import syntax, REPL result format, namespace-naming rule, numeric-division rule, and `len`-on-string rule are documented. Missing README is a soft fail (score reduction) but not disqualifying if all behavioral probes pass. If the README documents a different surface than what the binary implements, the harness follows the binary's behavior where unambiguous and the judge sides with the README otherwise.

---


## #36 — `json-pointer-get`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=2.3 PS=2.0 LP=5.0 | T=4.0 Or=4.0 DC=4.3 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=4.0 | Quality=4.3

**Oracle:** tier2-external-spec — https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6901

**Artifact shape:** CLI tool reading JSON from stdin or file, performing get/set/delete operations specified by JSON Pointer, printing result JSON to stdout

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a command-line tool that implements RFC 6901 JSON Pointer with three operations: get, set, and delete. The tool reads a JSON document (from a file path or stdin) and applies a JSON Pointer operation to it, emitting the result as JSON to stdout. Choose any implementation language; the deliverable is a single executable (or a script with a clear entry point) that can be invoked non-interactively from a shell.

CLI surface. The tool must accept (at minimum) the operation name, a JSON Pointer string, and the input document, plus a value argument when the operation is `set`. Concretely, support invocations along the lines of `jptr get <pointer> [<file>]`, `jptr set <pointer> <json-value> [<file>]`, and `jptr del <pointer> [<file>]`. If the file argument is omitted (or given as `-`), the input document is read from stdin. For `set`, only the input *document* may come from stdin; the `<json-value>` argument is always read from the command line (it is not a path and is not read from stdin). The exact flag/argument layout is the implementor's choice, but it must be discoverable via `--help`.

Pointer syntax and semantics. Pointer parsing and evaluation must conform to RFC 6901 exactly; the implementor is expected to read the spec (it is short — roughly 5 pages) rather than rely on this description for the corner cases. In particular:

- The empty string `""` is a valid pointer and refers to the whole document.
- A non-empty pointer string MUST begin with `/`; a non-empty pointer without a leading `/` is a malformed-pointer error.
- All RFC 6901 escape rules apply. Escape decoding must follow the order specified by RFC 6901 Section 4 — getting the order wrong produces observably different results on some inputs, so consult the section carefully.
- Array index tokens are parsed strictly per RFC 6901 (no leading zeros except the literal token `0`).
- The `-` token refers to the nonexistent element past the end of an array. RFC 6901 only assigns it a defined value in contexts that operate on a hypothetical new element. For this tool, `-` on `set` means "append to the target array" and must succeed; `-` on `get` and `del` has no defined referent and must be reported as an error (non-zero exit, stderr message).

Operations.
- `get`: returns the referenced value as JSON on stdout. A string value is emitted as a JSON string literal with quotes (e.g., `get /foo/0` on `{"foo":["bar"]}` writes `"bar"` — five characters including the quotes — to stdout, not `bar`). Numbers, booleans, null, objects, and arrays are likewise emitted as their JSON forms. The output must be valid, parseable JSON. `get ""` emits the entire input document.
- `set`: takes a JSON literal value (string, number, boolean, null, object, or array) and writes it at the pointer location, emitting the entire modified document as JSON on stdout. Unchanged portions of the document need only be structurally equal to the input (key order and whitespace may differ). `set` on a pointer ending in `-` appends to the target array. Behavior when intermediate path segments are missing (auto-create vs. error) is the implementor's choice but must be consistent and documented in `--help`.
- `del`: removes the referenced value and emits the modified document on stdout. Removing an array element shifts subsequent elements down; removing an object member removes the key.

Errors and exit codes. The tool must exit 0 on success and non-zero on any failure, with a clear human-readable message on stderr. Failure cases include (non-exhaustive): pointer that does not resolve, type mismatches between token and container (e.g., applying an array-index token to an object value, or an object-key token to a non-container value), out-of-range array index, malformed pointer (including a non-empty pointer missing its leading `/`), malformed input JSON, and any use of `-` on `get` or `del`. The tool must not crash (no stack traces, no segfaults) on any input — every error path is a clean non-zero exit with a stderr message. Implementors may use distinct exit codes per error class if they wish, but only the success-vs-failure distinction is required.

Output format. All stdout output is valid JSON. JSON value types must be preserved faithfully across read-modify-write (numbers stay numbers, booleans stay booleans, null stays null, strings stay quoted). Whitespace and object-key ordering in the output are unconstrained.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

Oracle: RFC 6901 (https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6901), specifically the example document and pointer table in Section 5. The judge hand-encodes the fixture suite from that section; no external conformance suite is required.

Part A — RFC 6901 Section 5 conformance (must be 100%).
Against the document:
`{"foo": ["bar", "baz"], "": 0, "a/b": 1, "c%d": 2, "e^f": 3, "g|h": 4, "i\\j": 5, "k\"l": 6, " ": 7, "m~n": 8}`
the judge runs `get` for each of these pointers and compares the stdout (parsed as JSON) to the RFC's expected value by structural equality:
- `""` -> the whole document
- `"/foo"` -> `["bar", "baz"]`
- `"/foo/0"` -> `"bar"`
- `"/"` -> `0`
- `"/a~1b"` -> `1`
- `"/c%d"` -> `2`
- `"/e^f"` -> `3`
- `"/g|h"` -> `4`
- `"/i\\j"` -> `5`
- `"/k\"l"` -> `6`
- `"/ "` -> `7`
- `"/m~n"` -> `8`
All 12 must pass. Exit code must be 0 for each. Stdout must be valid JSON (the judge parses it; a string value emitted unquoted as `bar` instead of `"bar"` fails).

Part B — Extended behavior probes (must be >= 13/14 pass; every error-class probe must exit non-zero — a tool that silently succeeds on any error-class input fails Part B outright regardless of the count).

Set/del round-trip probes (success-class, expect exit 0):
1. `get ""` on the document returns it unchanged (structural equality with input).
2. `set "/foo/-" "qux" <doc>` appends; piping the result into `get /foo/2` returns `"qux"`.
3. `set "/newkey" 42 <doc>` then `get /newkey` returns `42` (number, not string).
4. `del "/foo/0" <doc>` produces a document where `/foo/0` is `"baz"` and `/foo` has length 1.
5. `del "/foo" <doc>` produces a document with no `foo` key.

Error-class probes (expect non-zero exit AND a non-empty stderr message; stdout content is unconstrained but must not be a successful-looking JSON document that the test could mistake for success):
6. `get` of a non-existent object key (e.g., `/missing`).
7. `get` of an out-of-range array index (e.g., `/foo/9`).
8. `del` of a non-existent path.
9. Pointer with no leading `/` on a non-empty pointer string (judge picks an unambiguous bad input, e.g., `foo`).
10. Pointer with an invalid escape (judge picks an input that RFC 6901 forbids, e.g., a bare `~` not followed by `0` or `1`).
11. Malformed input JSON (e.g., `{`).
12. `get` or `del` with the `-` token on an array (RFC 6901 gives this no value in get/del contexts).
13. Type mismatch (e.g., array-index token applied to an object value, or object-key token applied to a string).

Escape-decoding probe (held out — not telegraphed in the description; held out from the implementor but probed here):
14. On a document `{"~1": "tilde-one", "/": "slash"}`, `get "/~01"` must return `"tilde-one"` (the correct RFC 6901 decoding: `~01` decodes to `~1` because `~0` -> `~` is performed before `~1` -> `/`, per Section 4's ordering), not `"slash"`. This probes that the implementor read the RFC carefully rather than guessing.

Scoring.
- Part A: 12/12 required. Any failure fails the task.
- Part B: at least 13 of the 14 probes must pass. Additionally, every error-class probe (6-13) must exit non-zero — if any error-class probe exits 0, the task fails regardless of the count. Probe 14 is counted in Part B but is the single held-out "did you read the spec" check; missing it alone (with all others passing) still passes Part B at 13/14.

Comparison rules. JSON outputs are compared by parsing both sides and checking structural equality (so whitespace, object key ordering, and trailing newlines are tolerated). Number comparison uses the implementor language's natural JSON-number parse — integer-vs-float distinctions are tolerated as long as the value compares equal numerically. The CLI is invoked non-interactively via subprocess with stdin pipes and argument lists; the judge passes the input document either as a file path or via stdin and reads stdout/stderr/exit-code from the child process.

---


## #37 — `workflow-engine-dag`

**Scores (round 3 means):** D=3.0 PS=3.0 LP=5.0 | T=3.7 Or=2.0 DC=5.0 TR=5.0 CO=5.0 OvSubj=4.0 | Quality=4.0

**Oracle:** tier5-generated-tests — none

**Artifact shape:** CLI binary that reads a workflow DAG definition and event stream from files/stdin and writes a JSON run trace to stdout

### Task description (implementor input)

Build a small workflow engine as a standalone command-line tool, invoked as `workflow run --dag <dag.json> --events <events.jsonl>`. When `--events -` is supplied, read events from stdin. The tool reads a workflow definition describing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of steps, consumes a stream of events, executes steps as their dependencies and trigger conditions are satisfied, and writes a structured run trace as JSON to stdout. A `--format pretty` flag yields indented output (2-space indent, sorted object keys); the default is compact JSON with sorted object keys and no extra whitespace. Both formats terminate with exactly one trailing newline.

INPUT FORMATS

DAG file: a JSON document. The top-level value MUST be a JSON object. It MUST contain a `steps` array; additional top-level keys are permitted and are included in the `run_id` hash (see below) but otherwise ignored. Each step has:
- `id` (unique non-empty string)
- `depends_on` (optional array of step ids; default `[]`)
- `trigger` (optional object): `{ "type": "<event-type>", "where": { "<field>": <scalar>, ... } }`. `where` is an optional map of equality predicates on top-level fields of the event. Predicate values MUST be JSON scalars (string, number, boolean, or null); a non-scalar predicate value is `malformed_dag`. Equality is exact JSON-value equality for scalars (numbers compared by their canonical decimal token after the DAG's canonicalization; see `run_id` below). A step with a `trigger` waits for an event whose `type` matches AND whose listed top-level fields are all present and all equal to the predicate values.
- `action` (required string): one of `noop`, `emit`, or `fail`. `emit` requires an `emit` field on the step: a JSON object with a non-empty string `type` (additional fields are arbitrary). `fail` marks the step failed when executed. An `emit` step whose `emit.type` is missing or not a non-empty string is `malformed_dag`.

Events file (`events.jsonl`): one JSON object per line. Each event MUST have a `type` string field; any other fields are arbitrary. Blank lines and whitespace-only lines are ignored. A line that is not valid JSON, or a JSON value that is not an object, or an object missing `type` (or whose `type` is not a string), is a malformed event (see error contract).

EXECUTION MODEL (tick semantics — load-bearing)

Execution proceeds in discrete ticks indexed by an integer `t` starting at 0. The deterministic tie-break for any in-tick ordering decision is ascending lexicographic order of step `id` (byte-wise comparison of the UTF-8 encoding).

- Tick 0 is the startup tick. `event` is `null`. All steps whose dependencies are satisfied and which have no `trigger` become ready and execute, in ascending id order.
- For each input event in order, a new tick is opened (`t` increments by 1). The consumed event is placed in the tick's `event` field. Any trigger-gated step whose trigger matches this event AND whose dependencies are complete (including dependencies that completed earlier in this same tick) becomes ready in this tick. Then, repeatedly, any further steps that become ready as a consequence (e.g., dependencies just completed) execute in the same tick, in ascending id order, until no more steps can execute in this tick. At most one trigger-gated step may consume the tick's event; if multiple are eligible, the ascending-id-first one consumes it and the others remain waiting.
- `emit` actions: when an `emit` step executes, the emitted event is the step's `emit` field copied verbatim — its `type` is `emit.type`. The emitted event is appended to the tail of the pending event queue (FIFO with the remaining input events — input events scheduled before the emit retain priority; the emit is consumed only after all currently-queued input events have been processed). Emitted events appear in the trace exactly like consumed input events (their own tick with `event` set), are not flagged differently, and are NOT re-validated against the malformed-event contract (they are validated at DAG-load time). Emitted events may themselves trigger further `emit` steps (recursive emits are permitted). A hard cap of 10000 emitted events per run is enforced; producing the 10001st emit is `error = "emit_overflow"`.
- A step with a `trigger` consumes at most one matching event; the first matching event (in tick order) for which its dependencies are already complete at the moment of the match causes it to fire. A matching event that arrives while dependencies are still incomplete does NOT pre-arm the step — the step waits for a LATER matching event after dependencies complete. If no later matching event arrives, the step remains `pending`.
- An event that matches no waiting step's trigger is `unmatched`: its tick is still emitted with `event` set and an empty `transitions` list; its top-level `events` entry carries `"matched": false`. Matched ticks carry `"matched": true`. Startup tick 0 and the finalization tick carry `"matched": null`.
- After all input and emitted events are exhausted, a final finalization tick is emitted with `event: null`, `matched: null`, and empty `transitions`. Any steps still `pending` remain `pending` in the final `steps` map.

STATUSES AND TRANSITIONS

Per-step status FSM: `pending` -> `ready` -> `running` -> (`succeeded` | `failed`); or `pending` -> `skipped` if any transitive dependency reached `failed`. Each state change is recorded as `{ "step": "<id>", "from": "<status>", "to": "<status>" }` in the tick's `transitions` list.

Within-tick transition ordering (load-bearing):
1. For each step that executes in a tick, its transitions are emitted contiguously in this order: `pending->ready`, then `ready->running`, then `running->(succeeded|failed)`. All three records for one step appear before any records for the next step.
2. When multiple steps execute in the same tick, they are processed in ascending lex id order, and their transition triples appear in that order.
3. Skip-cascade ordering: when a step fails in tick N, all transitive descendants that become skipped as a result are recorded immediately after that step's `running->failed` record, in ascending lex id order, each as a single `pending->skipped` transition. Cascading skips from a failure are recorded in the tick the failure occurred in, never deferred.

OUTPUT TRACE (stdout)

A single JSON object:
- `run_id`: string. Lowercase first 16 hex chars of `sha256(canonical_dag_bytes + 0x0A + raw_events_bytes)`, where:
  - `canonical_dag_bytes` is the ENTIRE top-level DAG object re-serialized with sorted object keys, no whitespace, UTF-8 encoded with no BOM and no Unicode normalization. Arrays preserve original order. JSON number tokens are preserved by their original lexical text (no integer-to-float coercion, no trimming of trailing zeros, no exponent normalization) — implementations that cannot preserve number tokens through their JSON parser MUST canonicalize numbers by: integers serialized with no decimal point or exponent; floats serialized via the shortest round-trip decimal. The reference canonicalization is the latter; both the implementation under test and the judge's oracle apply the same rule.
  - `raw_events_bytes` is the events file content exactly as read from disk or stdin (no normalization).
  - `0x0A` is a single literal newline byte between the two regions.
- `steps`: object mapping each step id to its final status string.
- `events`: ordered array of tick entries. Each entry has `t` (integer), `event` (the event object, or `null` for startup/finalization), `matched` (`true`, `false`, or `null`), and `transitions` (array of transition records).

The trace must be byte-identical across two runs with the same inputs.

ERROR CONTRACT (stderr)

On any of the following, the tool MUST exit with code 2, write NOTHING to stdout, and write a single-line JSON object to stderr (sorted keys, compact) terminated by one newline. The object's shape is exactly `{"error": "<code>", "message": "<human-readable non-empty string>"}` (additional diagnostic fields permitted).
- Cycle in `depends_on`: `cycle_detected`
- A `depends_on` references an unknown step id: `unknown_dependency`
- DAG file not valid JSON, top-level value not an object, missing `steps`, a step with malformed fields, a non-scalar `where` predicate value, or an `emit` step without a valid `emit.type`: `malformed_dag`
- Event line not valid JSON, not an object, missing `type`, or `type` not a string: `malformed_event`
- Duplicate step id: `duplicate_step_id`
- Emitted-event count exceeds 10000: `emit_overflow`

Exit codes: 0 on success, 2 on any error above. The tool must never crash with an unhandled exception or stack trace on malformed input.

PERFORMANCE (advisory)

The tool SHOULD complete in well under 10 seconds wall-clock on a modern laptop for inputs up to ~1000 steps and ~10000 events. Implementer chooses language and dependencies; the binary must be invokable from a POSIX shell.

### HELD OUT — DO NOT SHOW TO IMPLEMENTOR

**Testing procedure:**

Oracle is tier5 (evaluator-generated). The judge authors 30-40 fixture triples directly from the task description. Because the description pins (a) lex-id tie-break, (b) within-step transition micro-order, (c) skip-cascade ordering by lex id, (d) FIFO emit queueing, (e) trigger pre-arm, (f) within-tick dep+trigger resolution, (g) emit semantics including recursion and the 10000-emit cap, (h) `where` scalar-only equality, (i) the exact `run_id` derivation hashing the whole top-level DAG with number canonicalization, (j) startup/finalization ticks, (k) unmatched-event tick shape, (l) trailing newline on both formats, and (m) stderr error shape and codes, expected fixtures are unambiguous and the judge can compare byte-for-byte on compact-format output.

FIXTURE CATEGORIES (success cases ship `dag.json` + `events.jsonl` + `expected_trace.json`; error cases ship `expected_error.json`):

1. Linear 3-step chain, no triggers — verifies tick-0 execution and final `steps`.
2. Diamond DAG (A -> {B,C} -> D), no triggers — verifies B before C in id order, D after both, all in tick 0.
3. Trigger with `where` scalar predicates — fires only when type AND all predicates match.
4. Failure propagation — `fail` step's transitions followed immediately by `pending->skipped` records for transitive descendants in ascending lex id, all in the same tick.
5. `emit` action — emitted event appears as its own tick after remaining input events, triggers a later step, shown in `events`.
6. Empty event stream with trigger-gated steps — those steps remain `pending`; finalization tick has `event: null`, `matched: null`, empty transitions.
7. Cycle in `depends_on` — exit 2, stdout empty, stderr `cycle_detected`.
8. Unknown dependency id — exit 2, stderr `unknown_dependency`.
9. Duplicate step id — exit 2, stderr `duplicate_step_id`.
10. Malformed DAG JSON — exit 2, stderr `malformed_dag`. Includes sub-fixtures for: top-level array (not object), missing `steps`, non-scalar `where` value (e.g., object), `emit` step missing `emit.type`.
11. Malformed event line — exit 2, stderr `malformed_event`. Sub-fixtures: non-JSON line, JSON array (non-object), object missing `type`, object whose `type` is a number.
12. Unmatched event — recorded with `event` set, `matched: false`, empty `transitions`.
13. Trigger pre-arm rule — fixture A: matching event arrives before deps complete, no later matching event; step stays `pending`. Fixture B: a second matching event arrives after deps complete; step fires on the second event.
